name: Master of Architecture and Master of Infrastructure Planning

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This dual degree pairs the full professional M.Arch spine (studio sequence I-IV plus Advanced Studios, History, Construction, ECS, and Structures pairs, deepestPrereqChain 5) with an infrastructure-planning core (MIP 612 Environmental Policy, MIP 631 History and Theory of Infrastructure, MIP 673 Infrastructure Planning in Practice, MIP 674 Infrastructure and Architecture, MIP 675 Elements of Infrastructure Planning) and EPS 622 Sustainable Politics and Policy. The two halves are genuinely bridged, MIP 674 explicitly connects infrastructure and architecture, so the concept is coherent; what is missing is a parsed plan showing how the two degrees interleave and share credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an integrated dual-degree plan of study showing shared/double-counted courses and the term-by-term interleaving of the architecture and infrastructure tracks.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The architecture half has a strong, well-formed chain (depth 5, clean studio sequence), but missingCourseCount is 6: ARCH 507G, MARC 701, and crucially MIP 601, MIP 602, MIP 652, and MIP 655 are referenced yet absent from the catalog, so several infrastructure-core references dangle. That is a real structural defect for a dual degree whose value depends on both halves being complete and navigable.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or correct the six dangling references (ARCH 507G, MARC 701, MIP 601/602/652/655) so the infrastructure core and the architecture-thesis courses resolve to real catalog entries.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are stated for either degree component, so the combined competencies a dual-degree graduate should demonstrate are left implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish joint learning outcomes articulating how the degree integrates professional architectural design with infrastructure planning and policy.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan groups were parsed, so the combined credit load and, importantly, the credit savings a dual degree promises over pursuing the M.Arch and MIP separately are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable. Credit clarity matters most precisely for a dual degree.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total combined credits and which courses double-count, quantifying the savings versus the two standalone degrees.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The 31 course descriptions are rich and specific, but the page carries six dangling references and no integrated plan of study, so a reader cannot see how the architecture and infrastructure requirements combine into a single navigable program.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the dangling references and add an integrated dual-degree plan-of-study table with labeled terms and credit totals.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The combination is highly timely: amid major infrastructure investment and climate pressure, pairing professional architecture with environmental policy (MIP 612, EPS 622) and infrastructure planning/practice (MIP 673-675) produces a distinctive, market-relevant profile spanning design and public systems.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit resilient/green-infrastructure and infrastructure-finance content so graduates can lead on climate-adaptation and funding conversations.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions across both the ARCH and MIP courses are substantive and specific, e.g., MIP 675 enumerating roads, transit, community facilities, and drainage, and ECS I detailing passive systems; none read as placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Architecture-side prerequisites are stated as clear course codes, but the six dangling references (ARCH 507G, MARC 701, MIP 601/602/652/655) and the ARCH 542G prerequisite field polluted with description text reduce overall clarity and internal consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: The MIP core covers infrastructure fundamentals and policy but no course explicitly addresses climate-resilient or green/nature-based infrastructure, a central contemporary concern.
With MIP 673 covering planning practice, a dedicated course on public finance, P3s, and capital funding would complete the professional preparation for infrastructure delivery.
MIP 675 surveys systems including roads and transit, but no in-depth transportation/mobility-planning course exists despite its centrality to infrastructure.

missingTopics.topic: GIS and spatial analysis for infrastructure
missingTopics.rationale: The infrastructure half lacks a spatial-analysis/GIS course, a standard analytical tool for siting and network planning.
No quantitative systems-modeling or infrastructure-data course appears, limiting evidence-based analysis of complex networks.

emergingTrends.rationale: Adapting infrastructure to flooding, heat, and sea-level rise is a defining challenge the MIP core and architecture studios should jointly foreground.
Sensor-driven, digitally modeled infrastructure is emerging; MIP 673's practice course could incorporate digital-twin and monitoring approaches.
Infrastructure decarbonization (energy, transport, water) is a policy priority the environmental-policy and infrastructure courses should integrate.
MIP 674 (Infrastructure and Architecture) is well placed to expand toward landscape-integrated, nature-based infrastructure design.
Equitable distribution of infrastructure benefits is now central to policy; EPS 622 and the MIP core could add a distributional-equity strand.

topSuggestions: Resolve the six dangling course references (ARCH 507G, MARC 701, MIP 601/602/652/655) so both degree cores fully resolve.
Publish an integrated dual-degree plan of study with total combined credits and the double-counted courses that quantify the savings versus two standalone degrees.
State joint program learning outcomes integrating architectural design with infrastructure planning and policy.
Add resilient/green-infrastructure and infrastructure-finance courses to strengthen currency and professional readiness.
Clean the ARCH 542G prerequisite field and add a GIS/spatial-analysis course to support infrastructure analysis.
